topics.title: Introduction - Linear vs Non-Linear System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET743 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction - Linear vs Non-Linear System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Control System?
A **Control System** is an interconnection of components arranged in a definite configuration to provide a desired response to a set of inputs. It manages, commands, directs, or regulates the behavior of other devices or systems to achieve a specific objective.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, a system is broadly classified based on the nature of the governing equations that describe its dynamic behavior into **Linear Systems** and **Non-Linear Systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Linear System):**
> A system is said to be **linear** if it satisfies the **Superposition Principle**, which encompasses two sub-properties: **Additivity (Homogeneity)** and **Homogeneity (Scaling)**. Mathematically, a system represented by an operator $T$ is linear if and only if:
> $$T\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} = a \cdot T\{x_1(t)\} + b \cdot T\{x_2(t)\}$$
> for any arbitrary scalars $a$ and $b$, and any valid input signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Non-Linear System):**
> A system is classified as **non-linear** if its mathematical model contains at least one non-linear operator (such as multiplication, division, power, trigonometric function, saturation, dead-zone, hysteresis, or backlash) OR if the principle of superposition is violated. The governing differential equation will have coefficients that depend on the state variables, or contain non-linear terms like $x^2$, $\dot{x}^2$, $x \cdot \dot{x}$, $\sin(x)$, etc.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine two types of volume knobs on a stereo system:

- **Linear Knob (Ideal Volume Control):** If you turn the knob twice as much, the volume increases twice as much. The response is perfectly proportional and predictable. Whether the volume is at 10% or 80%, the relationship between the angle turned and the output volume remains constant. This is the essence of a **Linear System**.

- **Non-Linear Knob (Realistic Volume Control with Saturation):** When the volume is low, turning the knob a small amount produces a noticeable sound increase. But as you approach the maximum, the same small turn produces barely any change — the system has reached a "ceiling." Furthermore, at very low volumes, the system might not respond at all until a minimum threshold (dead zone) is crossed. This real-world knob behaves like a **Non-Linear System**.

### 1.3 Real-World Examples
**Linear System Examples:**
- Electrical circuits composed solely of **R (Resistors)**, **L (Inductors)**, and **C (Capacitors)** — modeled by linear differential equations.
- Ideal mass-spring-damper mechanical systems (Hooke's law region).

**Non-Linear System Examples:**
- **Servo motors** with saturation limits.
- **Thermostats** (relay-type / on-off control).
- **Hydraulic actuators** with dead zones and friction.
- **Satellite attitude control** with reaction wheel saturation.
- **Diode and transistor** circuits operating in non-linear regions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, the first module of PEAET743 focuses on establishing the **mathematical distinction** between linear and non-linear systems, classifying non-linearities, and motivating the need for dedicated non-linear analysis tools (Phase Plane, Describing Functions, Lyapunov Stability).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties of Linear Systems
A linear system must satisfy **ALL** of the following properties:

1. **Homogeneity (Scaling Property):** If the input is scaled by a constant $K$, the output is also scaled by the same constant $K$.
   $$T\{K \cdot x(t)\} = K \cdot T\{x(t)\}$$

2. **Additivity (Superposition Property):** The response to a sum of two inputs equals the sum of the individual responses.
   $$T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = T\{x_1(t)\} + T\{x_2(t)\}$$

3. **Combined Superposition Principle:** Together, these yield the linearity equation stated in Section 1.

4. **Time Invariance:** A system is **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** if its parameters do not change with time. The coefficients of the governing ODE must be constants (not functions of time).

5. **Causality:** The output at any time depends only on present and past inputs, not future inputs.

### 2.2 Mathematical Representation
**Linear ODE (Constant Coefficients):**
$$a_n \frac{d^n y}{dt^n} + a_{n-1} \frac{d^{n-1} y}{dt^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_1 \frac{dy}{dt} + a_0 y = b_m \frac{d^m u}{dt^m} + \cdots + b_0 u$$

**Non-Linear ODE (Variable Coefficients or Non-Linear Terms):**
$$\ddot{y} + a_1(y, \dot{y}) \dot{y} + a_0(y, \dot{y}) y + f(y) = u(t)$$

where $a_1$ and $a_0$ are functions of the state variables, and $f(y)$ is a non-linear function (could be $y^2$, $\sin(y)$, $e^y$, etc.).

### 2.3 Classification of Non-Linearities
Non-linearities are broadly classified into two categories:

**A. Inherent (Natural) Non-Linearities:**
- These are physical characteristics of the system's components that cannot be eliminated.
- Examples: Dead zone in motors, friction, saturation of magnetic flux, backlash in gears.

**B. Intentional (Artificial) Non-Linearities:**
- Deliberately introduced to improve system performance or achieve specific control objectives.
- Examples: Relay controllers (on-off control), non-linear damping for stability improvement.

### 2.4 Common Types of Non-Linearities

1. **Saturation:** The output increases linearly with input up to a threshold, then levels off.
2. **Dead Zone:** No output for small input magnitudes below a threshold.
3. **Backlash (Hysteresis in Gears):** Output remains constant while input changes within a small range.
4. **Relay (On-Off):** Output switches between two fixed values based on input sign.
5. **Coulomb Friction:** Output jumps discontinuously due to static friction.
6. **Square Law:** Output is proportional to the square of the input.
7. **Piecewise Linear:** Different linear segments joined at breakpoints.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Concept | Mathematical Form | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Linearity Test | $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = aT\{x_1\} + bT\{x_2\}$ | Must hold for all $a$, $b$, $x_1$, $x_2$ |
| Homogeneity | $T\{Kx\} = K \cdot T\{x\}$ | Scaling property |
| Additivity | $T\{x_1 + x_2\} = T\{x_1\} + T\{x_2\}$ | Superposition property |
| General Linear ODE | $\sum a_i y^{(i)} = \sum b_j u^{(j)}$ | $a_i, b_j$ are **constants** |
| General Non-Linear ODE | $F(t, y, \dot{y}, \ddot{y}, \ldots) = 0$ | $F$ is non-linear in $y$ or derivatives |
| Saturation | $y = \begin{cases} Kx, & \vert x \vert \leq S \\ K \cdot S \cdot \text{sgn}(x), & \vert x \vert > S \end{cases}$ | $S$ = saturation limit, $K$ = slope |
| Dead Zone | $y = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert x \vert \leq D \\ K(x - D \cdot \text{sgn}(x)), & \vert x \vert > D \end{cases}$ | $D$ = dead zone width |
| Relay | $y = M \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$ | $M$ = relay magnitude |
| Square Law | $y = K \cdot x^2$ | Pure quadratic non-linearity |
| Hysteresis (Backlash) | Output has memory; depends on input history | Width parameter $2h$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** The `vert` symbol is used in the table above to denote absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do NOT use the pipe character $\mid$ inside table cells.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Satellite Systems:** Reaction wheels exhibit saturation — control design must account for this.
- **Robotics:** Gear backlash must be compensated for precision motion control.
- **Power Systems:** Magnetic saturation in transformers affects voltage regulation.
- **Process Control:** Valve characteristics (equal percentage, quick-opening) are inherently non-linear.
- **Aerospace:** Aerodynamic forces (lift, drag) are non-linear functions of angle of attack and velocity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Testing a System for Linearity
**Problem:** Test whether the system described by $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ is linear.

**Step 1:** Apply input $x_1(t)$ to get output $y_1(t)$.
$$y_1(t) = T\{x_1(t)\} = t \cdot x_1(t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply input $x_2(t)$ to get output $y_2(t)$.
$$y_2(t) = T\{x_2(t)\} = t \cdot x_2(t)$$

**Step 3:** Form a linear combination of inputs $x(t) = a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)$.

**Step 4:** Compute the actual output due to the combined input.
$$y(t) = T\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\}$$

$$y(t) = t \cdot \{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\}$$

$$y(t) = a \cdot t \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot t \cdot x_2(t)$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the linear combination of individual outputs.
$$a \cdot y_1(t) + b \cdot y_2(t) = a \cdot t \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot t \cdot x_2(t)$$

**Step 6:** Verify equality.
$$y(t) = a \cdot y_1(t) + b \cdot y_2(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The system $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ is **Linear** (it satisfies superposition), but it is **Time-Varying** because of the explicit time dependence.

### 3.2 Derivation: Testing a System for Non-Linearity
**Problem:** Test whether the system described by $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is linear.

**Step 1:** Apply input $x_1(t)$ to get output $y_1(t)$.
$$y_1(t) = T\{x_1(t)\} = x_1^2(t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply input $x_2(t)$ to get output $y_2(t)$.
$$y_2(t) = T\{x_2(t)\} = x_2^2(t)$$

**Step 3:** Form a linear combination of inputs $x(t) = a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)$.

**Step 4:** Compute the actual output due to the combined input.
$$y(t) = T\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} = \{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\}^2$$

**Step 5:** Expand the squared expression.
$$y(t) = a^2 x_1^2(t) + 2ab \cdot x_1(t) x_2(t) + b^2 x_2^2(t)$$

**Step 6:** Compare with the linear combination of individual outputs.
$$a \cdot y_1(t) + b \cdot y_2(t) = a \cdot x_1^2(t) + b \cdot x_2^2(t)$$

**Step 7:** Verify equality.
$$y(t) \neq a \cdot y_1(t) + b \cdot y_2(t) \quad \text{(due to cross term } 2ab \cdot x_1 x_2\text{)}$$

**Conclusion:** The system $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is **Non-Linear**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Linear vs Non-Linear Systems

| Parameter | Linear System | Non-Linear System |
|---|---|---|
| **Governing Equation** | Linear ODE with constant coefficients | Non-linear ODE / algebraic equation |
| **Superposition** | Valid | Invalid |
| **Homogeneity** | Valid | Invalid |
| **Response to Sinusoid** | Sinusoid of same frequency (with possible phase shift) | Contains higher harmonics + sub-harmonics |
| **Stability** | Single equilibrium (typically) | Multiple equilibria, limit cycles possible |
| **Analysis Methods** | Laplace Transform, Transfer Functions, Root Locus, Bode, Nyquist | Phase Plane, Describing Function, Lyapunov |
| **Time Response** | Predictable, unique solution for given IC | Can be path-dependent, may exhibit chaos |
| **Frequency Content** | Input frequency preserved | Generates new frequencies (harmonics) |
| **Behavior Near Equilibrium** | Locally linearizable via Taylor expansion | Cannot be linearized globally |
| **Mathematical Tractability** | Well-developed, closed-form solutions exist | Numerical / graphical methods dominate |
| **Examples** | RLC circuit, ideal mass-spring | Saturated amplifier, relay, backlash |

### 3.4 Derivation: Mathematical Form of Saturation Non-Linearity
**Derivation of the piecewise linear saturation equation:**

Consider an amplifier with linear gain $K$ in the region $\vert x \vert \leq S$, and output clamped at $\pm K \cdot S$ beyond this region. The mathematical description emerges as follows:

**Step 1:** Define the linear region behavior.
$$y = K \cdot x, \quad \text{for } \vert x \vert \leq S$$

**Step 2:** Define the saturation region behavior.
$$y = K \cdot S, \quad \text{for } x > S$$
$$y = -K \cdot S, \quad \text{for } x < -S$$

**Step 3:** Combine using the signum function $\text{sgn}(x)$.
$$y = K \cdot x \quad \text{if} \quad \vert x \vert \leq S$$
$$y = K \cdot S \cdot \text{sgn}(x) \quad \text{if} \quad \vert x \vert > S$$

**Step 4:** Unified piecewise form.
$$y = \begin{cases} K \cdot S \cdot \text{sgn}(x), & \vert x \vert > S \\ K \cdot x, & \vert x \vert \leq S \end{cases}$$

This is the standard KTU textbook representation of saturation non-linearity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Linear vs Non-Linear Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LIN["LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEM"]
        R1[Reference Input r] --> SUM1((+))
        SUM1 --> CTRL1[Linear Controller Gc]
        CTRL1 --> PLANT1[Linear Plant G]
        PLANT1 --> OUT1[Output y]
        OUT1 --> H1[Sensor H]
        H1 --> SUM1
    end
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Non-Linear Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NL["NON-LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEM"]
        R2[Reference Input r] --> SUM2((+))
        SUM2 --> CTRL2[Controller]
        CTRL2 --> NLN[Non-Linear Element N]
        NLN --> PLANT2[Plant G]
        PLANT2 --> OUT2[Output y]
        OUT2 --> FB2[Feedback]
        FB2 --> SUM2
        NLN -. Inherent Saturation .-> PLANT2
    end
```

### 4.3 Classification Tree of Non-Linearities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Non-Linearities"]
    ROOT --> A["Inherent Natural"]
    ROOT --> B["Intentional Artificial"]
    A --> A1["Saturation"]
    A --> A2["Dead Zone"]
    A --> A3["Backlash Hysteresis"]
    A --> A4["Coulomb Friction"]
    A --> A5["Square Law"]
    B --> B1["Relay On-Off"]
    B --> B2["Bang-Bang Control"]
    B --> B3["Piecewise Linear Compensator"]
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow: Effect of Non-Linearity on Sinusoidal Input

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SIN["Sinusoidal Input x = A sin wt"] --> NL["Non-Linear Element N"]
    NL --> FFT["Frequency Spectrum"]
    FFT --> F1["Fundamental w"]
    FFT --> F2["3rd Harmonic 3w"]
    FFT --> F3["5th Harmonic 5w"]
    FFT --> F4["DC Component 0w"]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Block: Linear vs Non-Linear Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    COMP["System Comparison Framework"]
    COMP --> P1["Superposition Holds"]
    COMP --> P2["Superposition Fails"]
    P1 --> L1["Linear System"]
    L1 --> L2["Use Laplace Transform"]
    L2 --> L3["Use Transfer Function Analysis"]
    P2 --> N1["Non-Linear System"]
    N1 --> N2["Use Phase Plane Method"]
    N1 --> N3["Use Describing Function"]
    N1 --> N4["Use Lyapunov Stability"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - The linear system block shows a single controller-plant loop with no saturation indicators.
> - The non-linear system explicitly highlights the **Non-Linear Element (N)** as a separate block in series with the plant.
> - The signal flow diagram demonstrates how a pure sinusoid, when passed through a non-linearity, generates **harmonics** (3w, 5w, ...) — a phenomenon that NEVER occurs in a linear system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a linear system. State and explain the principle of superposition.**

**Model Answer:**
A system is said to be linear if it satisfies the **principle of superposition**. The principle of superposition has two parts:

**(i) Additivity:** The response of the system to a sum of inputs is equal to the sum of the responses to each input applied separately.
$$T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = T\{x_1(t)\} + T\{x_2(t)\}$$

**(ii) Homogeneity (Scaling):** If the input is multiplied by a constant $K$, the output is also multiplied by the same constant.
$$T\{K \cdot x(t)\} = K \cdot T\{x(t)\}$$

A system satisfying both these properties for all inputs and all constants is called a **linear system**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**List any four common non-linearities encountered in control systems and give one practical example for each.**

**Model Answer:**
The four common non-linearities are:

1. **Saturation** — Example: Amplifier output limited to a maximum voltage.
2. **Dead Zone** — Example: DC motor not rotating below a minimum applied voltage.
3. **Backlash** — Example: Gear train in a robotic arm where teeth engagement has clearance.
4. **Relay (On-Off)** — Example: Thermostat controlling room temperature with two-state switching.

(½ mark each for naming + ½ mark for example = 1 mark × 4 = 4 marks; total 3 marks with crisp presentation)

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With neat input-output characteristics, explain **saturation** and **dead zone** non-linearities. Derive their mathematical models. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why a non-linear system cannot be analyzed using the principle of superposition. Demonstrate with a suitable mathematical example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Saturation and Dead Zone Non-Linearities:**

**Saturation:**
- **Input-output characteristic:** Output $y$ increases linearly with input $x$ up to a saturation level $S$. Beyond this, the output remains constant at $\pm K S$.
- **Mathematical model:**

$$y = \begin{cases} K \cdot S, & x > S \\ K \cdot x, & -S \leq x \leq S \\ -K \cdot S, & x < -S \end{cases}$$

- **Physical example:** Magnetic saturation in a transformer core; output voltage of an operational amplifier.

**Dead Zone:**
- **Input-output characteristic:** Output $y$ is zero for $\vert x \vert \leq D$ (where $D$ is the dead zone width). Outside this region, the output varies linearly with input.

- **Mathematical model:**

$$y = \begin{cases} K(x - D), & x > D \\ 0, & -D \leq x \leq D \\ K(x + D), & x < -D \end{cases}$$

- **Physical example:** DC motor requiring a minimum threshold voltage to overcome static friction.

**Neat sketches (to be drawn in the exam):**
- Saturation: Two parallel horizontal lines for $\vert x \vert > S$ with a linear segment in between.
- Dead Zone: Flat horizontal line at $y = 0$ between $-D$ and $+D$, with sloped lines outside.

**(7 marks distribution:** 1.5 + 1.5 for definitions/diagrams, 2 for mathematical models, 1 for each example)

---

**(b) Why Superposition Fails for Non-Linear Systems:**

**Reason 1 — Mathematical Structure:** Non-linear systems have governing equations that contain non-linear terms (squares, products, trigonometric functions). When two inputs are summed and applied, the cross-product terms do not cancel or separate, violating the additivity condition.

**Demonstration Example:** Consider the system $y(t) = x^2(t)$.

Let $x_1(t) = 1$ and $x_2(t) = 2$. Then:
- $T\{x_1\} = 1^2 = 1$
- $T\{x_2\} = 2^2 = 4$
- $T\{x_1 + x_2\} = (1+2)^2 = 9$
- $T\{x_1\} + T\{x_2\} = 1 + 4 = 5$

Since $9 \neq 5$, the additivity property fails. Therefore, the system is **non-linear** and superposition is **invalid**.

**Reason 2 — Physical Reason:** Non-linear systems do not scale proportionally. A double increase in input does not produce a double increase in output. Furthermore, the response depends on the operating point (e.g., the system behaves differently in the saturation region than in the linear region).

**Consequence:** Since superposition fails, the powerful frequency-domain tools (Laplace transforms, Bode plots, Nyquist criterion) developed for linear systems cannot be directly applied. We must use specialized non-linear analysis methods like **Phase Plane Analysis**, **Describing Functions**, and **Lyapunov's Direct Method**.

**(7 marks distribution:** 2 for explanation, 3 for worked example, 2 for engineering consequences)

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Define a **linear time-invariant (LTI) system**. Explain the difference between a linear time-varying system and a linear time-invariant system with a suitable example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of neat block diagrams, compare the analysis approaches used for linear and non-linear control systems. Mention at least four analysis techniques specific to non-linear systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) LTI System Definition and Comparison:**

**Definition:** A **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system** is one that satisfies both the **superposition principle (linearity)** and the **time-invariance property**. Time-invariance means that if the input is shifted in time by $\tau$, the output is also shifted by the same $\tau$, with no change in shape.

$$T\{x(t - \tau)\} = y(t - \tau)$$

**Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) System Example:**
- An RLC circuit with constant $R$, $L$, $C$ values: $L \frac{di}{dt} + Ri + \frac{1}{C}\int i \, dt = v(t)$
- The coefficients are constants, so time-invariance holds. The system is also linear since superposition applies.

**Linear Time-Varying (LTV) System Example:**
- A mass-spring system where the spring constant changes with time: $m\ddot{x} + k(t)x = F(t)$
- For example, a spring whose stiffness $k(t) = k_0(1 + 0.5\sin(t))$ varies periodically.
- The system satisfies superposition (linearity in $x$) but NOT time-invariance.

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | LTI System | LTV System |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficients | Constant in time | Functions of time |
| Impulse response | Depends only on time difference $t - \tau$ | Depends on both $t$ and $\tau$ |
| Frequency response | Well-defined, single-valued | Not well-defined (changes with time) |
| Analysis | Transfer function methods applicable | Time-domain methods, time-varying transforms |

**(7 marks distribution:** 2 for LTI definition, 2 for LTV example, 1.5 for comparison table, 1.5 for distinguishing feature)

---

**(b) Comparison of Analysis Approaches:**

**Linear System Analysis Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Linear System Model] --> L2[Apply Laplace Transform]
    L2 --> L3[Derive Transfer Function]
    L3 --> L4[Root Locus]
    L3 --> L5[Bode Plot]
    L3 --> L6[Nyquist Criterion]
    L3 --> L7[State Space]
```

**Non-Linear System Analysis Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Non-Linear System Model] --> N2[Phase Plane Method]
    N1 --> N3[Describing Function]
    N1 --> N4[Lyapunov Stability]
    N1 --> N5[Popov Criterion]
    N1 --> N6[Circle Criterion]
    N1 --> N7[Bifurcation Analysis]
```

**Four Non-Linear Analysis Techniques:**

1. **Phase Plane Method:** A graphical technique plotting $\dot{x}$ versus $x$ (state versus rate) to visualize system trajectories, equilibrium points, and limit cycles. Most effective for second-order systems.

2. **Describing Function Method:** An approximate frequency-response technique where the non-linear element is replaced by a quasi-linear gain (describing function) that depends on input amplitude. Useful for predicting limit cycles.

3. **Lyapunov's Direct (Second) Method:** A general stability analysis method that uses an energy-like function (Lyapunov function) $V(x) > 0$ to prove stability without explicitly solving the differential equations.

4. **Popov's Criterion / Circle Criterion:** Frequency-domain stability criteria specifically designed for systems containing a linear part and a sector-bounded non-linearity. These extend the Nyquist criterion to non-linear systems.

**Key Difference:** Linear systems have a unique tool (transfer function) that handles all analyses uniformly. Non-linear systems require **multiple specialized techniques**, each suited to a particular class of non-linearity.

**(7 marks distribution:** 1.5 for each block diagram, 4 for four techniques (1 mark each), 1.5 for concluding comparison)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "linear" with "easy"**: Students often write "non-linear systems are hard to solve" without identifying the **mathematical property** (superposition failure) that makes them hard. Always anchor your answer to superposition.
> 2. **Forgetting time-invariance check**: In linearity tests, students check only homogeneity and additivity but forget that **LTI** requires time-invariance too. A time-varying system can still be linear.
> 3. **Incomplete Linearity Tests**: Just showing one input-output pair doesn't prove linearity. Students must show the test works for **arbitrary** $a$, $b$, $x_1$, $x_2$.
> 4. **Misidentifying Non-Linearities**: Saturation and dead zone look similar in sketches but have opposite characteristics near zero. Saturation is flat at the extremes; dead zone is flat near zero. **This is a 2-mark trap in Part A questions.**
> 5. **Marks lost on diagrams**: In Part B, students skip the input-output characteristic diagram, losing 1.5 to 2 marks. Always draw the characteristic.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Linearity is a property of the MATHEMATICAL MODEL, not the physical system itself.** Most "physical" systems are non-linear, but we often approximate them as linear within a small operating range.

✅ **The two pillars of linearity:** Homogeneity (scaling) + Additivity (superposition). Both must hold simultaneously.

✅ **A linear system can be time-varying.** A non-linear system can be time-invariant. These are independent properties.

✅ **Key test for non-linearity:** Apply two different inputs, sum the outputs, and compare with the output of the summed input. If they differ → non-linear.

✅ **Three pillars of non-linear analysis in KTU syllabus:** Phase Plane, Describing Function, Lyapunov — each addresses a different aspect (trajectory visualization, limit cycle prediction, stability).

✅ **Common non-linearities in KTU questions:** Saturation, Dead Zone, Backlash, Relay — know the input-output characteristic AND the piecewise mathematical equation for each.

✅ **Why not just linearize?** Linearization works only for small perturbations around an operating point. For large signals, design parameters (like saturation limits), and inherently non-linear phenomena (friction, backlash), the full non-linear analysis is essential.

✅ **The output of a non-linear system to a sinusoid contains harmonics.** This is the foundational concept behind the **Describing Function Method**.

✅ **Always use the signum function `sgn(x)` or piecewise notation** in mathematical models of non-linearities — this is the KTU board's preferred notation.

✅ **A relay system is a non-linear system that can produce limit cycles** — a phenomenon impossible in LTI systems.

✅ **For 14-mark Part B questions, always structure your answer as:** (i) Definition/Concept, (ii) Diagram/Characteristic, (iii) Mathematical Model, (iv) Example, (v) Application/Consequence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Nonlinearities in Control Systems
